text: The following events were reported: Flash Flood, Hail, Lightning, Thunderstorm Wind. These occurred near station USW00003927, approximately 38.7315 km away, between 2014-04-03 13:56:00 and 2014-04-03 20:25:00. The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.

The events included records with hailstones measuring 6.224615384615384 inches.Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 554023000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. These storms were hail producers and mainly affected areas primarily along and north of US 380 from Decatur to Sulphur Springs. The worst damage was done in Denton where hail up to the size of softballs caused close to $5 billion in damage. Three tornadoes also occurred with this system; an EF-0 in Collin County, an EF-0 in Hopkins County, and an EF-1 in Hunt County. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a dryline moving across the region. Event Narrative: Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Amateur radio spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Aubrey. Trained spotters reported ping pong ball sized hail. Quarter sized hail fell in Prosper. The hail near Aubrey grew to the size of baseballs, according to public reports which were relayed through the Aubrey Fire Department. Trained spotters reported quarter sized hail near Parvin. Quarter sized hail was reported in southern Denton. Numerous reports of tree damage in the city of Denton. A public report of nickel sized hail was received in Rhome. Amateur radio spotters reported trees knocked down in Krum. KDTO observed a wind gust near 80 mph. This storm continued from the airport into the southern sections of Denton, where several buildings were damaged. The Denton Record Chronicle reported firefighters from Sanger and Krum battled a gas well fire, which started due to lightning. Amatuer radio spotters reported nickel sized hail. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail near Frisco. Trained spotters reported golf ball sized hail which damaged a roof to a home and damaged two windows. The damage occurred near Legacy Road and State Highway 121. Amateur radio reported dime sized hail 2 miles west of Interstate 35 on Highway 380 in Denton, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a member of the broadcast media near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 380. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter near the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 288. Ping pong to baseball sized hail was reported by a member of the public in Denton. Trained spotters reported two inch diameter hail between Little Elm and Frisco. There was a public report of baseball sized hail 2 miles north of Denton. A public report of 3.5 inch diameter hail in Denton. Golf ball to hen egg sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 1.8 miles northeast of Denton. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by a member of the public approximately 2.5 miles north of Denton, TX at Hwy 288. A trained spotter reported tennis ball sized hail on the campus of Texas Woman's University in Denton. Golf ball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter 4 miles north of Denton, TX. Quarter sized hail was reported in northwest Fort Worth near Boat Club Road. Amateur radio spotters reported quarter sized hail in the Highland Village neighborhood. Quarter sized hail fell in Little Elm. Softball sized hail was reported by a trained spotter one-eighth of a mile west of the Texas Woman's Campus in Denton. Additionally, this storm covered much of the city of Denton, and a considerable part of the County as well. The Insurance Council of Texas estimated approximately 35,000 vehicles, and 22,000 homes were damaged in some fashion with this storm. The damage estimate is aproximately 500 million dollars. Quarter sized hail was reported by amateur radio 5 miles south of Krum. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Golf ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. The public also reported quarter sized hail in Denton at this time. Hen egg sized hail was reported by amateur radio in Denton, TX. Ping pong ball sized hail was reported by amateur radio east of Krum, TX. Numerous reports were received of six to eight inches of water on the road on the intersection of Hummingbird Lane and Cardinal Drive in the city of Denton. Amateur radio spotters reported Paloma Creek was out of its banks and flooded houses. Amateur radio operators reported half dollar sized hail 3 miles east of Denton.
